sections.text: Action begins at night as a sleepless, troubled King Kaitaba seeks counsel from a court favorite.

Their consultation reveals the problem: the king's warrior subjects have not been fulfilling their obligation to pay regular visits to the court. The councilor suggests sounding the royal drums at daybreak to see how the warriors answer the call.
At daybreak the drum sounds and one by one battalions attend, ask what the matter is, and receive a deferring response.
The king proceeds to the assembly point with regal mien and receives the gathered warriors' salutations.
Kaitaba asks why the warriors avoid him and is told the reason is a lack of sorghum — that is, there is no beer.

The king immediately sends an emissary to the king of Kiziba to request the use of land for growing sorghum. The emissary makes arrangements to have his travelling party received at his own house on the way.
The emissary's wife prepares for the travelers and receives them. They eat, are refreshed, and continue on their way.
They march northward through named landmarks.
They arrive at the palace, observe the royal protocol, and state the reason for their mission.
The king of Kiziba receives them well and grants their request. The travelling party thank him, return home, and report their success to Kaitaba.
Kaitaba summons his warriors and they go to cultivate the sorghum. When it ripens, some try to steal it, but Kaitaba uses a spiritual healer to draw off the sorcery that caused the bad behavior.

When the warriors drink the sorghum beer, they want to go out cattle raiding and encourage Kaitaba to allow them; but his answers to their bellicose praise speak only of peaceful matters. Other warriors devise a plan toward the same end: they will stir up hostilities with a neighboring king by concocting a report of slander.
They come to Kaitaba, greet him, and assert that several citizens of Kihanja Kingdom, including the king himself, have greatly insulted him.
Kaitaba becomes angry and seeks the counsel of his wife, who comes from Kihanja.
She warns him of the strength of the Kihanjan military, whereupon he questions her loyalty, but she insists she is loyal to him.

To prepare his raid, he engages diviners, but they see only a state of peace between Kaitaba's Kyamutwala and Kahigi's Kihanja. Still angered by the alleged insult, he sets out to raid Kihanja anyway; but once there he does not pursue violence.
The warriors renew their report of insult and add to it. Now enraged, Kaitaba informs his wife of his intent and then sets several villages on fire.
When Kihanja's King Kahigi hears of this, he offers tribute and hospitality in an attempt to assuage Kaitaba's anger, sending a messenger to negotiate.
The messenger delivers Kahigi's offer, and Kaitaba demands retribution for the putative insult.
Kahigi sends hundreds of cattle in hopes that Kaitaba will swallow his anger.
Kaitaba challenges the warriors who drive the cattle to him, but they are conciliatory. Kaitaba sends a parting message of warning to Kahigi.
The battle done, a warrior recites his self-praise, and in a coda the bard envisions the descendants of Kaitaba walking home after a battle.
